perturbation.original_text: Until the date that is eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, you will not, without the Company’s prior written consent, directly or indirectly solicit for purposes of employment, offer to hire or engage as a consultant, entice away or offer to enter into any contract with, or hire or engage as a consultant or enter into any contract with, during the period of employment or the 90-day period following such employment, any senior or key employee of the Company or its controlled affiliates who becomes known to you in connection with your evaluation of the Transaction; provided , however , that this paragraph will not prohibit you or any of your Representatives from engaging in any general advertising or general solicitation not targeted to any employees or former employees of the Company or its controlled affiliates, or from hiring any employees or former employees of the Company who respond to such solicitation.
perturbation.changed_text: Until the date that is five (5) years from the date of this Agreement, you will not, without the Company’s prior written consent, directly or indirectly solicit for purposes of employment, offer to hire or engage as a consultant, entice away or offer to enter into any contract with, or hire or engage as a consultant or enter into any contract with, during the period of employment or the 90-day period following such employment, any senior or key employee of the Company or its controlled affiliates who becomes known to you in connection with your evaluation of the Transaction; provided , however , that this paragraph will not prohibit you or any of your Representatives from engaging in any general advertising or general solicitation not targeted to any employees or former employees of the Company or its controlled affiliates, or from hiring any employees or former employees of the Company who respond to such solicitation.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the non-solicitation period to five years may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade in some jurisdictions, particularly for lower-level employees or where the restriction is overly broad.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements, and a five-year non-solicitation agreement could be seen as an unlawful restraint on trade if it effectively prevents employees from seeking employment elsewhere. California courts scrutinize such clauses to determine if they operate as de facto non-compete agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from 18 months to five years. The Perturbation Explanation states this could be an unreasonable restraint of trade. The Law Explanation states that California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements, and a five-year non-solicitation agreement could be seen as an unlawful restraint on trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing BPC 16600, states that every contract that restrains someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is void. The extension to five years, therefore, creates a potential contradiction by unlawfully restraining trade under California law.

perturbation.original_text: In that case, or if at any time the Company so requests in writing, you will promptly (and in any event within 10 days) either, at your option, (i) deliver to the Company all of the Evaluation Material (including all copies, reproductions, summaries, analyses or extracts thereof or based thereon) in your possession or in the possession of any of your Representatives or (ii) destroy or cause to be destroyed all such Evaluation Material in your possession or in the possession of any of your Representatives (such destruction to be acknowledged by you in writing to the Company).
perturbation.changed_text: In that case, or if at any time the Company so requests in writing, you will promptly (and in any event within 3 days) either, at your option, (i) deliver to the Company all of the Evaluation Material (including all copies, reproductions, summaries, analyses or extracts thereof or based thereon) in your possession or in the possession of any of your Representatives or (ii) destroy or cause to be destroyed all such Evaluation Material in your possession or in the possession of any of your Representatives (such destruction to be acknowledged by you in writing to the Company).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the time frame for returning or destroying confidential information to 3 days may not provide sufficient time for compliance, especially if large amounts of data need to be processed. This can increase the risk of non-compliance, particularly considering the scope of ‘Evaluation Material’.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) - Article 17 (Right to Erasure)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly contradicting Article 17, a 3-day window for data return/destruction could be insufficient to ensure full compliance with GDPR's "right to be forgotten", particularly if evaluation material includes personal data requiring secure and complete deletion. This is applicable, if Randstad Holding NV has any EU-based clients and processes the personal data. The time allowed must be reasonable and allow for complete and verifiable deletion.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: f the specific situation of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises, this Regulation includes a derogation for organisations with fewer than 250 employees with regard to record-keeping. In addition, the Union institutions and bodies, and Member States and their supervisory authorities, are encouraged to take account of the specific needs of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises in the application of this Regulation. The notion of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises should draw from Article 2 of the Annex to Commission Recommendation 2003/361/EC ( 5 ) . (14) The protection afforded by this Regulation should apply to natural persons, whatever their nationality or place of residence, in relation to the processing of their personal data. This Regulation does not cover the processing of personal data which concerns legal persons and in particular undertakings established as legal persons, including the name and the form of the legal person and the contact details of the legal person. (15) In order to prevent creating a serious risk of circumvention, the protection of natural persons should be technologically neutral and should not depend on the techniques used. The protection of natural persons should apply to the processing of personal data by automated means, as well as to manual processing, if the personal data are contained or are intended to be contained in a filing system. Files or sets of files, as well as their cover pages, which are not structured according to specific criteria should not fall within the scope of this Regulation. (16) This Regulation does not apply to issues of protection of fundamental rights and freedoms or the free flow of personal data related to activities which fall outside the scope of Union law, such as activities concerning national security. This Regulation does not apply to the processing of personal data by the Member States when carrying out activities in relation to the common foreign and security policy of the Union. (17) Regulation (EC) No 45/2001 of the European Parliament and of the Council ( 6 ) applies to the processing of personal data by the Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies. Regulation (EC) No 45/2001 and other Union legal acts applicable to such processing of personal data should be adapted to the principles and rules established in this Regulation and applied in the light of this Regulation. In order to provide a strong and coherent data protection framework in the Union, the necessary adaptations of Regulation (EC) No 45/2001 should follow after the adoption of this Regulation, in order to allow application at the same time as this Regulation. (18) This Regulation does not apply to the processing of personal data by a natural person in the course of a purely personal or household activity and thus with no connection to a professional or commercial activity. Personal or household activities could include correspondence and the holding of addresses, or social networking and online activity undertaken within the context of such activities. However, this Regulation applies to controllers or processors which provide the means for processing personal data for such personal or household activities. (19) The protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data by competent authorities for the purposes of the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties, including the safeguarding against and the prevention of threats to public security and the free movement of such data, is the subject of a specific Union legal act. This Regulation should not, therefore, apply to processing activities for those purposes. However, personal data processed by public authorities under this Regulation should, when used for those purposes, be governed by a more specific Union legal act, namely Directive (EU) 2016/680 of the European Parliament and of the Council ( 7 ) . perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the timeframe for compliance (returning/destroying data) from 10 days to 3 days. According to the Law Explanation, this timeframe may be insufficient to comply with GDPR's 'right to be forgotten,' especially if the evaluation material contains personal data that requires secure and complete deletion. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Art. 17 GDPR, states that the data controller shall erase personal data 'without undue delay' where the data subject withdraws consent or the data are no longer necessary. Reducing the time to 3 days could be seen as creating a situation where it's difficult to comply with the requirement of 'without undue delay', particularly if the company has a large amount of data to process. This makes the changed contract contradict the requirement of erasing data without undue delay.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of fifteen (15) months from the date of this Agreement (the “Assessment Period”), you and your Representatives (acting on behalf of you or your subsidiaries) will not, directly or indirectly, and you will cause any person or entity controlled by you or acting in concert with you not to, without the prior written consent of the Board of Directors of the Company, (i) in any manner acquire, agree to acquire or make any proposal to acquire, directly or indirectly, any securities or property of the Company or any of its subsidiaries, or any securities or contract rights the terms or value of which are dependent on securities of the Company
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date of this Agreement (the “Assessment Period”), you and your Representatives (acting on behalf of you or your subsidiaries) will not, directly or indirectly, and you will cause any person or entity controlled by you or acting in concert with you not to, without the prior written consent of the Board of Directors of the Company, (i) in any manner acquire, agree to acquire or make any proposal to acquire, directly or indirectly, any securities or property of the Company or any of its subsidiaries, or any securities or contract rights the terms or value of which are dependent on securities of the Company
perturbation.explanation: Extending the standstill period to five years could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade or an anti-competitive practice, particularly if it unduly restricts Randstad Holding NV's ability to engage in otherwise legitimate business activities.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1 of the Sherman Act prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade. While a standstill agreement is not per se illegal, a five-year period, depending on the specific facts and circumstances, could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it significantly impairs Randstad's ability to compete in the market or makes the target company less accessible to other potential acquirers. The law is applied under a 'rule of reason' analysis.

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the standstill period from 15 months to 5 years. The Law Explanation states that a five-year period, depending on the circumstances, 'could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint,' triggering scrutiny under Section 1 of the Sherman Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 1, declares illegal 'every contract, combination...in restraint of trade or commerce.' The change introduces a potentially unreasonable restraint of trade, violating the Sherman Act, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The validity and interpretation of this Agreement will be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York, without giving effect to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity and interpretation of this Agreement will be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of Delaware, without giving effect to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware when the agreement is between a Dutch company and involves a transaction regarding a company located elsewhere (potentially outside of Delaware) could create issues regarding which state has the most significant relationship to the transaction, potentially leading to disputes over enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 addresses choice of law clauses in contracts. While courts generally honor these clauses, they may not be enforced if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no reasonable basis for the parties' choice. Applying Delaware law without a reasonable connection to Delaware might lead a court to apply the law of a state with a more significant relationship, such as New York where the company is located, or where the negotiations took place, rendering the choice-of-law clause unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from New York to Delaware, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, creates a potential contradiction. The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, as referenced in the Law Explanation, suggests that choice-of-law clauses may not be enforced if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or transaction. While the scraped snippets do not directly address this Restatement, the general principle is that a contractual term, such as the choice of law, should have a reasonable basis. Choosing Delaware without such a basis could lead a court to disregard it, resulting in the application of a different state's law, thereby contradicting the explicitly stated choice in the contract.
